summary:
The Konbaung Dynasty (1752–1885 AD) was the last ruling dynasty of Burma (Myanmar), founded by Alaungpaya who reunified Burma after the collapse of the Restored Toungoo Dynasty, and creating the most powerful Burmese state in history — one that destroyed [[event:2422|Ayutthaya]] (1767), conquered significant Chinese and Indian territories, and ultimately came into fatal conflict with the expanding [[event:105|British Empire]]. Alaungpaya (r. 1752–1760) rose from the headman of a small village to unify most of Burma within eight years through military genius and political skill, defeating the Mon-backed Restored Hanthawaddy Kingdom and founding a new capital at Shwebo. His campaigns against Siam, though ultimately unsuccessful in his own lifetime, set the stage for his successors' destruction of Ayutthaya.

The dynasty's military power reached its peak under King Hsinbyushin (r. 1763–1776), who destroyed Ayutthaya and repelled four Chinese invasions (1765–1769) — the only Southeast Asian ruler to successfully resist the Qing dynasty's military expansionism. The failed Chinese invasions exhausted both sides and resulted in a peace that normalized Sino-Burmese relations. The Konbaung expansionist impulse eventually brought Burma into conflict with British India: three Anglo-Burmese Wars (1824–1826, 1852, 1885) progressively dismantled Burmese sovereignty — the First War cost Burma Assam and the coastal Tenasserim and Arakan provinces; the Second War cost lower Burma; the Third War in 1885 overthrew the last Konbaung king, Thibaw, and annexed all of Burma as a province of British India, ending Burmese independence for over 60 years.
